evidence: A 40-year-old man is evaluated in the pre-operative holding area this morning for a planned right shoulder arthroscopy with possible labral repair, a procedure the orthopedic team anticipates will last less than ninety minutes under general anesthesia. Three weeks ago he was involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which a burst fracture at the T11 vertebral level produced complete motor and sensory loss below the waist; neurosurgery stabilized the injury with a thoracolumbar brace but elected to defer open fixation because alignment remained acceptable and the patient was hemodynamically stable. Since that admission he has remained on the inpatient rehabilitation service, spending nearly all of his time supine or in a specialized wheelchair, and over the last four days the nursing flow sheet documents no more than assisted pivot transfers from bed to chair and back, with otherwise continuous bed rest, so he has effectively been immobilized for well over 72 hours. Because of poor venous access in his upper extremities after multiple blood draws and intravenous medications, a right internal jugular triple-lumen catheter was placed under ultrasound guidance twelve days ago; the insertion site is clean, dry, and secured with an occlusive dressing, and there have been no fever spikes or culture growths to suggest infection. The patient has not required intubation or vasopressors, has had no episodes of systemic inflammatory response, bloodstream infection, or lung consolidation, and his temperature curve has remained within normal limits. He denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, exertional chest pain, or prior episodes of decompensated heart failure. Review of respiratory symptoms is similarly benign, with clear breath sounds on daily examinations and no supplemental oxygen need. He has not undergone any other operative intervention or sedation since the original trauma evaluation, and there is no history of fractures to the pelvis, hip, or long bones, nor has he worn a fiberglass cast. The lower limbs, which he cannot voluntarily move, are warm and well perfused but have become puffy to mid-calf over the last week; circumferential measurements taken yesterday show a two-centimeter increase compared with baseline, and pitting edema is present to the anterior shins, though there is no discoloration, superficial venous engorgement, or calf tenderness on squeeze test, and he reports never having been told he has varicose veins. Duplex imaging has not yet been performed, and he denies prior clots, chest pain, hemoptysis, or unexplained shortness of breath at any time in the past. He recalls no relatives with blood disorders or thrombotic events, and a thrombophilia panel drawn years ago for unrelated research purposes was reportedly unremarkable; that evaluation specifically included genotyping for the Factor V Leiden mutation, which was negative. Past medical history is otherwise limited to well-controlled mild hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily; he has never had myocardial infarction, chronic lung disease, bowel inflammation, or malignancy. He does not smoke, drinks beer only on football weekends, and uses no recreational drugs. Medications include lisinopril, gabapentin for neuropathic pain, and a daily bowel regimen; no hormonal therapy, erythropoietin, or glucocorticoids are in use. Physical examination shows an alert man lying supine on the transport stretcher, speaking comfortably. Vital signs: blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg, heart rate 72 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8 °C, oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated, the precordial impulse is normal, and S1 and S2 are crisp without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Breath sounds are vesicular throughout with no wheezes or crackles. The abdomen is soft and non-distended with active bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Both lower extremities are flaccid with 0/5 strength, absent deep tendon reflexes, and intact passive range of motion; skin is intact without pressure ulcers. Moderate bilateral ankle and pretibial edema is appreciated, and pedal pulses are palpable with Doppler. Upper extremity neurologic and motor function is full. The central line dressing is intact with no erythema or induration. Laboratory studies drawn yesterday reveal hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, platelet count 255 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.8 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, creatinine 0.96 mg/dL, AST 24 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, fasting glucose 102 mg/dL, prothrombin time 12.2 s, INR 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 29 s. Chest radiograph taken four days ago for routine line placement confirmation shows clear lung fields and proper catheter tip position in the superior vena cava. A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrates normal sinus rhythm without ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Airway assessment reveals adequate mouth opening, full neck extension, Mallampati class II, and no dental issues that would complicate intubation. He has been nil per os since midnight, the operative consent has been verified, and sequential compression sleeves have been ordered but are not yet applied. No pharmacologic prophylaxis has been administered prior to incision as the anesthesia and surgical teams await completion of formal venous thromboembolism risk stratification.
